mnemonics.title: Truth Table for Connectives
mnemonics.content:
N(ot): T→F, F→T
A(nd): T∧T=T, else F
O(r): F∨F=F, else T
I(mplies): F→T=T, else F
Iff: Same=T, Diff=F
Mnemonic: 'TNT' for NOT (Toggle Not Thing), 'AND needs All True', 'OR needs One True', 'IF fails only when T then F'.

mnemonics.title: Remembering Quantifier Negation
mnemonics.content:
Flip the quantifier and negate the predicate: 'NOT ALL' becomes 'SOME NOT', 'NONE' becomes 'ALL NOT'. Think: Flip → Negate.
¬∀ → ∃¬
¬∃ → ∀¬

mnemonics.title: Remembering Modus Forms
mnemonics.content:
MODUS PONENS (MP): 'Affirm' → 'Conclude'
p→q, p ∴ q
Modus TOLLENS (MT): 'Deny' → 'Deny'
p→q, ¬q ∴ ¬p
Mnemonic: 'Ponens' sounds like 'positive/put', Tollens sounds like 'take away/toll'.

mnemonics.title: Conditional Equivalences
mnemonics.content:
p → q ≡ ¬p ∨ q (Material Implication)
Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p (Always equivalent)
Converse: q → p (NOT equivalent to original)
Inverse: ¬p → ¬q (NOT equivalent to original)
Remember: Original and Contrapositive are EQUIVALENT; Converse and Inverse are EQUIVALENT to each other.

acronyms.expansion: Truth Table For Five connectives (T,F for each) - 2^n rows for n variables
acronyms.usage: Remember: 1 variable = 2 rows, 2 variables = 4 rows, 3 variables = 8 rows

acronyms.expansion: Predicate, Existential, Singleton/Universal, Order, Scope - key concepts in predicate logic analysis
acronyms.usage: When analyzing predicate logic statements, check each component

acronyms.expansion: Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Hypothetical Syllogism, Disjunctive Syllogism - common valid argument forms
acronyms.usage: Common valid argument forms to recognize in logical proofs

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with 2^n rows for n propositions. Fill each column systematically: for 1st proposition T/F alternates every 1 row, 2nd every 2 rows, 3rd every 4 rows. Think of binary counting from 0 to 2^n-1.
Think of p → q as a one-way street from p to q. It's blocked (FALSE) only when you go from TRUE (p) to FALSE (q). Otherwise you can travel. The arrow points from antecedent to consequent.
∀ looks like an upside-down A = 'All' (A for All). ∃ looks like backwards E = 'Exists'. Draw circles: ∀ = big circle containing ALL elements, ∃ = at least one dot somewhere in the domain.

Two propositions p ≡ q can be visualized as having identical 'truth patterns' - their columns in truth table are identical. Draw overlapping circles like a Venn diagram to show they cover the same cases.

analogies.analogy: Like a light switch - each proposition is either ON (true) or OFF (false). Connectives are like wiring multiple switches together. A tautology is like a circuit that's always lit regardless of switch positions.

Like a search query with placeholders. 'x is greater than 5' is like a template: '___ is greater than 5'. Filling x with 7 gives true, with 3 gives false. Quantifiers are like 'find ALL matches' (∀) or 'find ANY match' (∃).

A valid argument is like a recipe: if you follow the steps (premises) exactly, you MUST get the result (conclusion). The recipe works IF ingredients are correct. Sound argument is when ingredients are actually good.

Original: 'If it rains (p), ground gets wet (q)'. Contrapositive: 'If ground is NOT wet (¬q), then it did NOT rain (¬p)'. This is like proving absence by looking at what SHOULD happen if the cause existed.
